paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: LVMH is the 15th largest company in the world today by market cap. It is the only company in that top 15 that is not technology or oil besides Berkshire Hathaway. Their market cap has grown 20x in 20 years. They own 75 houses today that include Dior, Louis Vuitton, Moet, Hennessy, Veuve Clicquot, Dom Pérignon, Tiffany. This wide sweeping empire is owned and controlled by the now wealthiest man in the world, Bernard Arnault.

paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: Fascinatingly, this richest man in the world wasn't the founder of any of these brands. Mr. Arnault turned $15 million of capital in 1985 into the over $200 billion fortune that it is today. The luxury industry is like business strategy bizarro world. You need scarcity. You can't lower your cost structure too much without devaluing your brand. All the lessons that we've learned on previous episodes, it's kind of like the exact opposite of what will show up today.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: We start in Paris in 1946, immediately after the end of World War Two. Into that time and place, steps Christian Dior. Before the war, he had been a designer at various Parisian fashion houses. In February 1947, he showed his first collection, and it literally changed the world. It was a complete repudiation of not just the Nazi wartime aesthetic, but wartime period. The pieces used tons of fabric, luxury fabrics. This was a radical statement. After the war where there's rationing on fabrics, these new pieces that are embracing life, embracing luxury in 1947, this is a radical statement.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: By 1949, Dior fashions were literally 75% of Paris's fashion exports and 5% of the entire nation's export revenue. In 1950, Jacques Rouet came up with a new business model idea. He thought, this name, this brand has so much value. What if we license the use of the brand out to other goods producers? The Dior label would get licensed to hundreds of third parties. There was a Dior license label out there. Thousands of products manufactured everywhere in the world at every different level of quality, very few of which were actually created by Christian Dior.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: In 1957, Christian Dior dies suddenly of a heart attack very unexpectedly. There was no concept of fashion labels as existing beyond the person and the artistic director. These labels and houses did not survive the death of their founders. But there's one kid who's got a lot of talent. His name is Yves Saint Laurent. They promote this kid, Saint Laurent, to artistic director at age 21. And almost as much as Christian Dior revolutionized the world with the new look 10 years earlier, Saint Laurent does the same thing again.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Unfortunately for Boussac, after three years of Yves Saint Laurent running Dior, Boussac forces him out in 1960. They basically never recover from this. For basically the next 15, 20 years, this is just a cash cow that they're milking. Meanwhile, though, the rest of the Boussac empire is basically going down the tubes. By the late 60s, they're losing like $20 million a year. In 1978, the whole Boussac group files for bankruptcy in what was the largest bankruptcy in French national history.

paragraphs.speaker: ben
paragraphs.name: Ben Gilbert
paragraphs.text: Enter one Bernard Jean Etienne Arnault. Bernard was born in 1949 in Roubaix. His dad ran a company called Ferret Savignel, which was a quite successful civil engineering firm. Bernard's growing up steeped in running this engineering family business. He ends up going for college to the very prestigious École Polytechnique. In 1971, he graduates and he goes to work back in the family business. Five years later, his father's like, you're groomed to take over. The keys are yours.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: Bernard convinces his father that the civil engineering business is not a great growth business and that they should start to transition into real estate development. Under Bernard's new leadership, they sell off the old industrial construction division. They find a successful niche building vacation homes, second houses, at the French Riviera. Then the 1980s come along. Francois Mitterrand comes to power and it becomes much more of a socialist country. They enact a wealth tax in France. Bernard moves his family to America. He develops Palm Beach condos.

paragraphs.speaker: david
paragraphs.name: David Rosenthal
paragraphs.text: His next door neighbor happens to be a guy named John Kluge, who at the time was the wealthiest person in America. Kluge was in the middle of doing the largest LBO ever at that point in time, taking Metro Media private. Bernard is fascinated by him. He's like, tell me of these leveraged buyouts. He's just blown away. He wants to do that. Bernard puts the word out.
